sound_type: caterwauling -- wild ascending screaming sequences; barking, cackling, howling; chaotic multi-voice layering; 300-2000+ Hz; E=6 maximum energy; the most alarming owl sound in the PNW forest
ecosystem: Forest, resident. Dense canopy, mature and old-growth. Caterwauling during pair interactions, territorial disputes, courtship. Year-round, peaks breeding season.
season: Year-round resident. Caterwauling peaks February-April (breeding season) and during territorial disputes. Can occur any time of year. Primarily nocturnal, dusk and pre-dawn peaks.

coordinates.description: Barred Owls caterwaul throughout PNW forests. Caterwauling reported from forests, parks, and suburban areas with large trees.

taxonomy_note.description: Second Barred Owl entry (first: degree 137, 'Who cooks for you?' hooting, E=4). FOURTH SPECIES WITH DOUBLE ENTRIES after Pileated Woodpecker (108-109), American Dipper (116-117), and Great Horned Owl (135-136). E=6 maximum energy -- the most dramatic energy step in any double entry (+2, from E=4 to E=6). Fourth consecutive Strigidae (135-138).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: fifth entry total (Spotted Owl 4, GHO 135, GHO pair duet 136, Barred Owl 137, Barred Owl caterwauling 138). Four consecutive at 135-138.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Same as degree 137. Strix from Greek 'screech owl.' varia from Latin 'variegated.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Strix -- second entry (first at 137). The caterwauling is the second Strix contribution.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Fifth entry. Four consecutive at 135-138.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae continues for a fourth consecutive degree. Longest family streak in the engine.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 300-2000+ Hz. Dramatically wider than hooting (400-550 Hz). Spans multiple octaves from bass howls to high-pitched screams.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 500-1500 Hz. The screaming sequences concentrate energy in the mid-to-high range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Caterwauling spans 300-2000+ Hz. Individual screams may peak at 1000-1500 Hz. Barks and howls at lower end (300-600 Hz).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual sequences: 5-30 seconds. Full bouts: 1-10+ minutes. Intensity varies throughout.
song_metrics.note_structure: CATERWAULING: Wild ascending screams ('AAAH-aaah-AAAH!'), sharp barks ('WAH! WAH!'), rapid cackling ('ah-ah-ah-ah'), drawn-out howls ('HOOOOO-AWWW'). Chaotic, unpredictable, overlapping when multiple birds involved.
song_metrics.note_types: Ascending screams, percussive barks, manic cackling, bass howls. All components may overlap and interleave unpredictably.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No regular phrase structure. Chaotic, ascending, accelerating. Sequences build in intensity and pitch before subsiding. Multiple birds create dense overlapping texture.
song_metrics.timbre: Raspy, throat-tearing, anguished. Radically different from the rich, resonant hooting. The caterwauling timbre is rough, intense, and alarming. Often described as 'demonic' or 'primate-like.'
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6. MAXIMUM ENERGY. Significantly louder than hooting. Audible 500+ meters through dense forest. The loudest regular nocturnal vocalization in the PNW forest.
song_metrics.tempo: Irregular, accelerating, unpredictable. Bursts of screaming interspersed with brief pauses. The tempo builds and subsides in waves.
song_metrics.context: Pair bonding/courtship (primary), territorial disputes, agitation/excitement, nest defense. The caterwauling is a high-arousal vocalization indicating intense behavioral engagement.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal variation in caterwauling structure across range. The chaotic quality is consistent. Individual and pair variation in intensity and duration.
song_metrics.key_property: CATERWAULING -- E=6 MAXIMUM ENERGY. Wild ascending screaming, barking, cackling, howling. 300-2000+ Hz (dramatic range). Fourth double-entry species. Most dramatic energy step: E=4 to E=6 (+2). Fourth consecutive Strigidae (135-138). Most alarming owl sound in PNW. Triggers human primal alarm response. 'Someone is being murdered in the woods.' The hidden wild voice of the measured owl.

plumage.overall: Same as degree 137. Brown and white barred pattern. Dark brown eyes. No ear tufts.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Same as degree 137. Dark eyes, barred chest, no ear tufts.

physical_description.overall: Same as degree 137. Length: 43-50 cm. Weight: 500-1050 g.
physical_description.field_id: If you hear wild screaming, barking, and cackling in the forest at night -- especially if it sounds like 'someone being murdered' or 'monkeys' -- it is almost certainly Barred Owls caterwauling. Listen for the ascending pitch pattern.

nodes.title: Caterwauling -- E=6 Maximum Energy
nodes.description: Wild ascending screaming sequences. 300-2000+ Hz. Raspy, chaotic, alarming. The most intense owl vocalization in the PNW. Triggers primal human alarm response. E=6 -- maximum energy. The highest energy in the four-degree Strigidae streak.

nodes.title: Fourth Species with Double Entries (137-138)
nodes.description: Pileated WP (108-109, +1), American Dipper (116-117, +1), GHO (135-136, +1), Barred Owl (137-138, +2). Fourth double-entry species. Most dramatic energy step: E=4 to E=6, bypassing E=5.

nodes.title: Human Alarm Response -- Primal Fear
nodes.description: The caterwauling triggers primal alarm in humans. 'Someone is being murdered.' 'Monkeys in the forest.' 'Demonic.' Police reports of mysterious screaming. Night amplification. The most psychologically impactful owl vocalization.

nodes.title: Fourth Consecutive Strigidae (135-138)
nodes.description: GHO solo (135, E=4), GHO pair (136, E=5), Barred Owl hooting (137, E=4), Barred Owl caterwauling (138, E=6). Four consecutive. Energy arc: 4, 5, 4, 6. V-shaped then surge. Longest family streak in the engine.

cross_references.relationship: DOUBLE ENTRY! Hooting (137, E=4) to caterwauling (138, E=6). The most dramatic energy step in any double entry: +2. Same species, radically different acoustic outputs. Measured speech vs. wild screaming.
STRIGIDAE QUARTET! GHO (135-136) + Barred Owl (137-138). Four consecutive Strigidae. Two species, two double entries. Energy arc: 4, 5, 4, 6.
DOUBLE ENTRY PATTERN! Pileated (108-109, +1), Dipper (116-117, +1), GHO (135-136, +1), Barred Owl (137-138, +2). Four species. The Barred Owl shows the most dramatic contrast.
STRIGIDAE FAMILY! Five Strigidae entries total. Spotted Owl (4), GHO solo (135), GHO pair (136), Barred Owl hooting (137), Barred Owl caterwauling (138). The most represented family.
